section.title: The Cross Alone
section.sectionRationale: Paul takes the pen in his own hand to close the letter, and his final words gather up its whole message. The agitators want to boast in the flesh and avoid persecution for the cross; Paul will boast in nothing except the cross of Christ, through which the world has been crucified to him. What counts is not circumcision but a new creation. He blesses all who walk by this rule and bears on his body the marks of Jesus.
section.thesis: Everything in the letter comes to rest on the cross: the agitators seek to boast in the flesh and escape the cross's offense, but Paul boasts only in the cross of Christ, by which he is dead to the world, for what counts is not circumcision but the new creation, and grace and peace rest on all who live by this rule.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the true motives of the agitators, who compel circumcision to avoid persecution and to boast in the flesh; Paul's counter boast, only in the cross of Christ, through which the world is crucified to him and only new creation counts; and the closing blessing on all who walk by this rule, with the marks of Jesus and the grace of Christ.

units.label: The agitators' true motive
units.note: compelling circumcision to avoid the cross

verses.text: See what large letters I am using to write to you with my own hand!
Those who want to make a good impression outwardly are trying to compel you to be circumcised. They only do this to avoid persecution for the cross of Christ.
For the circumcised do not even keep the law themselves, yet they want you to be circumcised that they may boast in your flesh.

units.label: Boasting only in the cross
units.note: the world crucified to me, a new creation

verses.text: But as for me, may I never {{kauchaomai|boast}}, except in the {{stauros|cross}} of our Lord Jesus Christ, through which the world has been crucified to me, and I to the world.
For neither circumcision nor uncircumcision means anything. What counts is a new {{ktisis|creation}}.

units.label: Peace on all who walk by this rule
units.note: the marks of Jesus and the grace of Christ

verses.text: Peace and mercy to all who walk by this rule, even to the Israel of God.
From now on let no one cause me trouble, for I bear on my body the marks of Jesus.
The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit, brothers. Amen.

kauchaomai.gloss: To boast, to glory in, to find one's confidence and pride in something. The agitators boast in the flesh, in numbers circumcised. Paul refuses every such boast and glories in one thing only, the cross of Christ. What a person boasts in reveals what they trust for their standing; Paul's whole hope and honor rest on Christ crucified, not on any achievement of his own.

stauros.gloss: Cross, the instrument of Roman execution and, for Paul, the center of the gospel. The cross is the one thing worth boasting in, for there Christ bore our curse and won our salvation. It is also the offense the agitators try to avoid, since a crucified Savior leaves no room for boasting in the flesh. Through the cross the world and Paul are dead to each other.

ktisis.gloss: Creation, that which is created; here new creation, the new reality God brings about in Christ. Neither circumcision nor uncircumcision matters at all; what counts is that a person is made new by God's re-creating power. The gospel is not a religion of external marks but the dawn of the new creation, begun in those who belong to Christ and moving toward the renewal of all things.

basic.context: Paul takes the pen to write the ending himself, and he sums up everything. The agitators push circumcision to look good and to escape being persecuted for the cross. Paul wants to boast in only one thing, the cross of Christ, which has made him dead to the world and the world dead to him. Outward marks count for nothing; what matters is being a new creation. He blesses all who live by this and signs off with grace.
basic.hermeneutics: These are Paul's closing, summarizing words, written large in his own hand for emphasis. Read them as the point of the whole letter distilled: the cross alone, not the flesh, is the ground of boasting, and the gospel produces new creation, not new rule keeping.
basic.originalLanguages: The agitators kauchaomai, boast, in the flesh, but Paul boasts only in the stauros, the cross, of Christ. Through it the world is crucified to him. What counts is not circumcision but a new ktisis, creation, the new reality God makes in Christ.
basic.typology: The marks of Jesus that Paul bears, the scars of suffering for Christ, echo the sufferings of the servant and mark Paul as belonging to his crucified Lord. The new creation fulfills the prophetic promise of new heavens and a new earth, begun now in Christ and consummated at the end.
basic.christ: The cross of our Lord Jesus Christ is the center and climax of the letter. It is the one ground of boasting, the place where the world's power is broken, and the source of the new creation. Christ crucified saves, and to add circumcision is to flee the offense of His cross. The letter ends where it must, with grace from the Lord Jesus.
basic.god: God is the maker of the new creation, the one who re-creates people in Christ so that outward marks mean nothing. He grants peace and mercy to all who walk by this rule. The whole letter has defended the freeness of His grace, and it closes with that grace pronounced over the readers.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 1 says Paul preaches Christ crucified and boasts only in the Lord. 2 Corinthians 5 says anyone in Christ is a new creation. Philippians 3 counts all gain as loss for the sake of Christ.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible moves toward the cross and the new creation. Here Paul gathers both: he glories only in the cross, and he declares that what counts is being made new. The story that began in a broken creation ends in a new one, secured by Christ crucified and pronounced over all who live by this rule.

context.literary: Paul's closing, written in his own large handwriting, functions as a summary and final appeal rather than a routine farewell. It returns to the letter's central themes, circumcision, the flesh, the cross, and boasting, and drives them to a climax in verses 14 and 15 before the blessing. The personal note about the marks of Jesus seals his authority.
context.historical: The agitators' desire to avoid persecution suggests that preaching circumcision kept them in favor with the wider Jewish community and out of trouble, while the offense of the cross drew hostility. Paul, by contrast, carried the physical scars of suffering for Christ, the marks of Jesus, evidence of a very different allegiance.
context.canonical: The passage crystallizes the theology of the cross that runs through Paul's letters, boasting only in Christ crucified, and it introduces the theme of new creation that Paul develops elsewhere. It stands as one of Scripture's clearest statements that the gospel produces a new reality, not merely a reformed religion.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the self serving motives of the agitators, to declare that the only ground of boasting is the cross of Christ, to affirm that what counts is the new creation and not circumcision, and to pronounce grace and peace on all who live by this gospel.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the agitators' motives and Paul's own stance, and it prescribes the rule by which believers are to walk: boasting only in the cross and living as new creations. The closing blessing applies the gospel of grace to the readers.

debates.question: Who is the Israel of God in verse 16?

views.label: All who walk by this rule, Jew and Gentile in Christ
views.case: The most natural reading takes the Israel of God as the whole believing community who live by the gospel of new creation, the true Israel defined by faith in Christ, consistent with the letter's argument that the children of Abraham are those of faith.

views.label: Believing ethnic Israelites
views.case: Some read it as a distinct blessing on Jewish Christians within the church, a subset of those who walk by this rule.

debates.bearing: Either way the blessing rests on those who live by the gospel of the cross and new creation; the letter has already shown that true Abrahamic sonship is by faith in Christ.

debates.question: What are the marks of Jesus that Paul bears?

views.label: Scars from suffering for Christ
views.case: The marks are the wounds and scars Paul received in persecution and hardship for the gospel, branding him as belonging to and serving Christ.

views.label: A spiritual identification with Christ's sufferings
views.case: Some read them more broadly as Paul's participation in the sufferings of Christ, though the language points to physical marks on his body.

debates.bearing: Paul's scars, borne for Christ, stand in pointed contrast to the mark of circumcision the agitators prized; his body testifies to a costly allegiance to the crucified Lord.

study.originalLanguages: The closing turns on three words. The agitators kauchaomai, boast, in the flesh, glorying in how many they have persuaded to be circumcised, for what a person boasts in shows what they trust. Paul will boast in one thing only, the stauros, the cross of Christ, the place where salvation was won and where the world's power over him was broken, so that he and the world are dead to each other. And the sum of the whole letter is that neither circumcision nor uncircumcision counts, but only a new ktisis, creation, the new reality God brings about in Christ. The gospel is not a rearranged religion of outward marks but the dawn of God's new world.

typology.fulfillment: Paul's scars for Christ echo the sufferings of the servant and mark him as belonging to his crucified Lord, in contrast to the mark of circumcision.
Fulfills the prophetic promise of new heavens and a new earth, begun now in those who are in Christ and consummated in the renewal of all things.

study.christ: The cross of our Lord Jesus Christ is the center and climax of the entire letter, and Paul makes it his only boast. There Christ bore the curse, won salvation, and broke the power of the present evil age, so that through the cross the world is crucified to Paul and he to the world. To add circumcision is to flee the offense of that cross and to look for a boast in the flesh. Fittingly, the letter that fought for the gospel of grace ends with the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ pronounced over its readers.
study.god: God is the Creator who now makes all things new in Christ, so that being a new creation is what finally counts, not any outward mark. He grants peace and mercy to all who walk by this rule, the true Israel of God defined by the gospel. The whole letter has been a defense of the freeness of His grace, and it closes with that grace, from the Lord Jesus Christ, resting upon the spirits of the believers.

crossReferences.note: Christ crucified as the power and wisdom of God, so that the one who boasts boasts in the Lord.
If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation, matching verse 15.
Counting all things loss for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ, boasting only in Him.
The promise of new heavens and a new earth, the new creation begun in Christ.

study.oneStory: The whole Bible moves toward the cross and the new creation, and Paul's final words gather both. He glories in nothing but the cross of Christ, where the old world's grip was broken, and he declares that what counts is being made new. The story that opened with a good creation fractured by sin closes with a new creation secured by Christ crucified, and the grace of the Lord Jesus is pronounced over all who walk by this rule.

sermon.title: Boasting in the Cross (Galatians 6:11 to 18)
sermon.why: Keller brings the letter to its climax in the cross, showing why Paul boasts in nothing else, how the cross crucifies the world to the believer, and why the gospel produces new creation rather than boasting in the flesh.

quiz.q: Why were the agitators compelling the Galatians to be circumcised?

options.text: To make a good impression and avoid persecution for the cross.
options.note: Verse 12 exposes their self serving motive.

options.text: Out of sincere love for the Galatians' souls.
options.note: Paul says their motive was to avoid persecution and boast in the flesh.

options.text: Because they themselves kept the whole law perfectly.
options.note: Verse 13 says they did not even keep the law themselves.

options.text: Because God had commanded it for salvation.
options.note: The whole letter denies that circumcision is required for salvation.

quiz.q: In what does Paul say he will boast?

options.text: Only in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ.
options.note: Verse 14 names the cross as his single boast.

options.text: In the number of converts he has made.
options.note: That is the agitators' boast in the flesh, not Paul's.

options.text: In his own record of law keeping.
options.note: Paul counts such things as nothing; he boasts in the cross.

options.text: In his sufferings as an achievement.
options.note: He bears the marks of Jesus, but his boast is the cross of Christ.

quiz.q: What does Paul say truly counts?

options.text: A new creation, not circumcision or uncircumcision.
options.note: Verse 15 states the sum of the letter.

options.text: Being circumcised as a mark of belonging.
options.note: Circumcision means nothing; new creation is what counts.

options.text: Keeping the calendar of special days.
options.note: Outward observance does not count; new creation does.

options.text: Impressing others outwardly.
options.note: That is the agitators' aim, which Paul rejects.

quiz.q: What has the cross done, according to verse 14?

options.text: Through it the world has been crucified to Paul, and he to the world.
options.note: The cross breaks the world's hold on the believer.

options.text: It has made the law the way of salvation.
options.note: The cross ends boasting in the flesh, not establishes law as salvation.

options.text: It has left Paul still enslaved to the world.
options.note: The opposite; he is now dead to the world through the cross.

options.text: It has become a reason to boast in the flesh.
options.note: The cross removes all boasting in the flesh.

quiz.q: How does Paul end the letter?

options.text: With grace and peace on all who walk by this rule, and the grace of Christ.
options.note: Verses 16 and 18 close with blessing and grace.

options.text: With a final demand that they be circumcised.
options.note: He closes with grace, not a demand for circumcision.

options.text: With a curse on the whole church.
options.note: The curse was on those who distort the gospel; he blesses the faithful.

options.text: With a boast in his own accomplishments.
options.note: He boasts only in the cross and blesses them with grace.

questions: Paul boasts in nothing but the cross. Honestly, what are you most tempted to boast in or find your identity in, and how does the cross reframe it?
Through the cross the world was crucified to Paul. What hold does the world's approval still have on you, and what would being dead to it look like?
What counts is a new creation, not outward marks. Where are you tempted to measure your faith by externals rather than by God's re-creating work?
The agitators wanted to avoid the offense of the cross. Where are you tempted to soften the gospel to escape opposition or fit in?
The letter ends with grace be with your spirit. As you finish Galatians, how has the message of grace apart from works landed on your own heart?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes through which can also be rendered through whom; either way the cross of Christ is the means by which the world is crucified to Paul.
The Israel of God is most naturally read as all who walk by this rule, the believing community defined by faith in Christ, in keeping with the letter's argument about the true children of Abraham.
The marks of Jesus are the scars Paul bore from suffering for Christ, branding him as belonging to his Lord, in pointed contrast to the mark of circumcision.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Galatians 6:11-18. No em dashes in this passage required substitution. Sermon verified this session from the Gospel in Life series Galatians: New Freedom, New Family by Tim Keller.
